jsonLdWeb.name: Seed Starting Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate seed starting dates for indoor sowing, transplanting, and direct sowing based on USDA zone or last frost date.

mainEntity.name: When should I start seeds indoors?

acceptedAnswer.text: Count back from your last frost date by the weeks needed for each crop. Tomatoes need 6-8 weeks indoors, peppers 8-10 weeks, broccoli 6-8 weeks, and lettuce 4-6 weeks before transplanting outdoors.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my last frost date?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your last frost date is based on your USDA hardiness zone. Zone 3 is typically late May, zone 5 is mid-April to early May, zone 7 is mid-March to early April, and zones 9-10 are January-February.

mainEntity.name: When can I transplant seedlings outdoors?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most warm-season crops (tomatoes, peppers, squash) should be transplanted after all danger of frost has passed — typically 1-2 weeks after your last frost date. Cold-hardy crops can go out 2-4 weeks before the last frost.

mainEntity.name: Which seeds should be direct sown?

acceptedAnswer.text: Root crops (carrots, beets, radishes), beans, peas, corn, squash, and cucumbers do best direct sown because they dislike root disturbance. Sow after last frost for warm-season crops or 2-4 weeks before for cool-season ones.

mainEntity.name: What USDA zone am I in?

acceptedAnswer.text: USDA zones range from 1 (coldest, -60°F) to 13 (warmest, 65°F+). Check the USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map at planthardiness.ars.usda.gov by entering your zip code for your exact zone.

mainEntity.name: Can I start seeds too early?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Starting too early leads to leggy, root-bound seedlings that struggle when transplanted. Follow the recommended weeks-before-frost timing for each crop. Peppers (8-10 weeks) need the longest indoor start.

howItWorks:
How the Seed Starting Calculator Works

This calculator uses your USDA zone or last frost date to count back the recommended weeks for indoor seed starting and forward for transplant and direct sow dates.

Indoor Start = Last Frost Date − Weeks Before Frost
  • Tomatoes: Start 6-8 weeks before last frost, transplant after frost
  • Peppers: Start 8-10 weeks before, transplant 1-2 weeks after frost
  • Broccoli: Start 6-8 weeks before, transplant 2-4 weeks before frost
  • Lettuce: Start 4-6 weeks before, can direct sow 2-4 weeks before frost
  • Beans/Peas: Direct sow only, near or after last frost
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

When should I start seeds indoors?

Count back from your last frost date by each crop's requirement: tomatoes 6-8 weeks, peppers 8-10 weeks, broccoli 6-8 weeks, lettuce 4-6 weeks. This gives seedlings enough time to grow strong before going outside.

How do I find my last frost date?

Your USDA zone determines your average last frost date. Zone 6 averages April 15, zone 7 is April 1, zone 5 is late April. Enter your zone above or look up your zip code on the USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map.

When can I transplant seedlings outdoors?

Warm-season crops like tomatoes and peppers should go out 1-2 weeks after the last frost. Cold-hardy crops like broccoli and lettuce can be transplanted 2-4 weeks before the last frost date.

Which seeds should be direct sown?

Root crops (carrots, beets, radishes), beans, peas, corn, squash, and cucumbers prefer direct sowing. They dislike root disturbance from transplanting. Sow after frost for warm-season, before frost for cool-season crops.

What USDA zone am I in?

Zones range from 1 (coldest) to 13 (warmest). Most of the continental US falls between zones 3 and 10. Check planthardiness.ars.usda.gov with your zip code for your exact zone.

Can I start seeds too early?

Yes. Starting too early creates leggy, root-bound plants that struggle at transplant time. Stick to the recommended timing for each crop. If seedlings outgrow their pots, move to larger containers.

faq.question: Is the Seed Starting Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.
